21.1.label: Question 21.1
21.1.scenario:
List and define the types of reserve held to meet the claims liabilities of the following insurers

and, with reasons, give indications of their sizes.

(i).question:
A long‐term insurer selling only conventional individual annual premium critical illness
policies.

Reserves for conventional critical illne: Reserves in total will be small for this business, ...                                                 [½]
... since it is a protection contract with little need to build up a fund.                             [½]

However, if the benefits are very likely to be paid (ie claim frequencies are high), then reserves could be larger.                                                                                   [½]
This is the discounted value of future net cash out‐flows ...                                          [½]
... ie the expected present value of future claims plus expenses less premiums ...                     [½]

... and it will be the majority of the reserves, since the funds plus premiums are held to meet the benefits paid out in the future.                                                                  [½]
This is the discounted value of claims that have arisen but have not yet been settled ...              [½]
... ie the expected present value of claims plus expenses.                                             [½]
This includes:          claims that have been diagnosed, but not yet reported (IBNR), ...                            [½]
... which could be substantial if claims take time to be reported                            [½]
         claims that have been reported, but not yet settled, ...                                     [½]
... which should be small unless there are significant delays in paying claims due to a lengthy claims verification process for certain illnesses.                                   [½]

This is in respect of additional costs that need to be set aside for the eventuality that a particular option becomes more valuable if it is exercised than not.                                            [½]
Such options might include the option to increase the sum insured or extend the term of the policy.                                                                                     [½]

(ii).question:
A health and care insurer selling only individual private medical insurance business, where
premiums are paid annually and cover is renewable every year.

Reserves for PMI: Unearned premium reserve (UPR)                                                                            [¼]
This is the balance of premiums received in respect of periods of insurance not yet expired.              [½]
If risk is reasonably uniform and business is written uniformly throughout the year, this will be approximately half the annual written premium, ...                                                [½]
... and so the size depends on the volumes of business written.                                           [½]
Unexpired risk reserve (URR) / additional unexpired risk reserve (AURR)                                   [¼]
The URR is the total reserve in respect of the unexpired cover, and will be greater than the UPR if premiums are inadequate to meet future claims and expenses.                                     [½]
The AURR is the excess of the URR over the UPR.                                                           [½]
This should be small, unless there have been significant shortcomings in recent premium rates. [½]
Incurred but not reported (IBNR)                                                                          [¼]

This is a reserve in respect of claims that have arisen but which have yet to be notified to the insurer.                                                                                                  [½]

This depends on how quickly policyholders notify the insurer (which may depend on the insurer’s pre‐authorisation procedures), but should be small if most claims have to be notified in advance of receiving health and care treatment.                                                        [½]
Claims in transit                                                                                         [¼]
This is a reserve in respect of claims reported, but not assessed, or not recorded.                       [½]
The size depends on the benefits provided and type of treatment claimed for.                              [½]

For example, this could be sizeable if there are a significant number of claims for emergency medical treatment, where claims have been reported to get access to treatment, but the insurer has not had the chance to assess the claim.                                                   [½]
Outstanding (reported) claims reserve                                                                     [¼]
This is a reserve in respect of claims notified to the insurer but not yet fully settled.                 [½]

Very few claims will be long tailed as chronic conditions are normally excluded, so this reserve will probably be small.                                                                               [½]
However, if there are valid claims, such as those requiring prolonged treatment or observation, then the reserve could be significant.                                                          [½]
Incurred but not enough reported                                                                       [¼]

This is a reserve representing changes to the case estimates for reported claims. It is held when, for example, not all details have yet been submitted and a provision needs to be established for the remainder.                                                                                  [½]

This should be reasonably small since reported claims will probably have had a reasonably accurate initial estimate of treatment cost established at the agreement of health and care provision.                                                                                            [½]
However, for some potentially large claims (eg cancer treatment), the initial estimate could be dramatically inaccurate, resulting in a large adjustment (either positive or negative).         [1]
All of the above claims reserves should have an allowance for claims expenses (and if not, then a separate reserve should be held).                                                               [½]
Equalisation and catastrophe reserves                                                                  [¼]
These are reserves where it is felt that the current year is atypical and amounts will have to be held back for abnormal events.                                                                    [½]
Catastrophe reserves should be very small indeed since catastrophes that lead to significant claims are very unlikely for PMI.                                                                     [½]

The size of equalisation reserves should be small since experience is not particularly uncertain or volatile.                                                                                         [½]
However, this could vary depending on exactly what conditions are covered and the desirability of smooth results (eg whether the company is mutual or proprietary).                             [½]
The extent of both reserves will depend on prevailing legislation regarding the taxation and use of such reserves.                                                                                  [½]
Investment mismatching                                                                                 [¼]
In some territories there may be a regulatory requirement hold an explicit investment mismatching reserve.                                                                                  [½]
The size depends on the extent to which the assets held do not match the liabilities.         [½]
[Maximum 10]

21.2.label: Question 21.2
21.2.scenario: A health insurer sells only annual premium private medical insurance business.

Unexpired risks: These are risks in respect of the portion of policies written that lies beyond the reserve calculation date, for which premiums have already been received.                                                [1]

(ii).question:
Discuss the factors the insurer should consider when calculating its reserves for unexpired
risks.

Factors to consider: The allowance for unexpired risk will consist of an unearned premium reserve (UPR), and if necessary, an extra allowance called the additional unexpired risk reserve.                                [½]
The two will cover the allowance for both future claims and associated expenses on the unexpired portion of cover.                                                                             [½]
An assumption as to how business is sold throughout the year will be needed.                               [½]
In the absence of any other information, it would be reasonable to assume that PMI business is sold evenly throughout the calendar year.                                                      [½]
An assumption on the length of cover provided by the policies is also needed.                              [½]
As the premiums are annual, it is (again) likely that cover is one year long.                              [½]
An alternative, and more accurate approach, would be to use the actual unexpired period calculated on a policy‐by‐policy basis, ...                                                                [½]
... in which case the last two assumptions would not be required.                                          [½]
Next, an assumption on the earnings of the premium, ie how the risk on the policy is spread over the year, would be needed.                                                                    [½]
One option would be to assume that it is even over the year.                                               [½]
The UPR in this case would be a simple proportion of the written premium based on the number of days cover unexpired (either assumed or actual, as above) as at the calculation date.   [½]
However, it is perhaps better argued that there will be more claims over the winter period (eg for pneumonia), in which case the risk is greater over those months.                                [½]
For example, in the UK, the months of November to February might give more risk exposure.                  [½]
The UPR can be adjusted to allow for this by using a monthly approach to the calculation, putting more risk weight on the winter months.                                                          [½]

There is also an increase of risk with age, as health deteriorates with age and claims are more likely at the end of the policy year. However, over the space of one year, this variation in risk is not great and could be taken to be negligible.                                                       [½]
The purpose of the reserving exercise would need to be considered.                                         [½]
For example, if it is for statutory purposes, a prudent method might be used, whereas for internal purposes, a best estimate basis might be preferred.                                             [½]

An approximate allowance for prudence could be built in, for example, by taking a larger premium for the calculation (even the full written premium, so that when the proportion to calculate the UPR is taken, the result would be larger).                                                      [½]
For an ongoing company, the UPR should be reduced for the uneven incidence of expenses over the year.                                                                                 [½]

This will lower the reserve, the desirability of which depends again on the purpose of the calculation.                                                                                              [½]
Finally, the reserve should be increased if necessary to allow for inadequate premiums, giving the additional unexpired risk reserve.                                                              [½]
In order to assess this, the adequacy of past premiums should be analysed.                           [½]
[Maximum 8]

(i).question:
State when case estimation is more suitable than statistical estimation of claims
              reserves.

Case estimation is more suitable than st:          sufficient qualitative data available for each individual claim … … and an experienced team of claims assessors
 high variance in the claim amo:          no appropriate statistical model.                                   [½ mark each, maximum 3]

(ii).question:
List the factors that a claims assessor would consider when estimating the ultimate claims
cost for an individual private medical insurance claim.

Factors to consider when estimating the:          name of surgeon, consultant or other medical principal                                          [½]
         policy coverage (full indemnity, excess, limits, recuperation benefit etc)                      [½]
         age, gender and past claims history of claimant may have some bearing                           [½]

21.4.label: Question 21.4
21.4.scenario: A health insurer sells only income protection contracts.
21.4.question: List the information needed to accurately calculate its reserves for reported claims.

For each claim, the following data are n:          any limits on payment terms (durations or amounts)                                               [¼]
         details of changes to be made to the benefit over time                                           [¼]
         other information to assess likely duration of claim, eg:                                        [¼]
–         details of any previous claims (amounts and durations)                                 [¼]
Also:          external data on likely duration of sickness (eg reinsurers’ data)                               [¼]
         details of the reserving basis (eg discount rate)                                                [¼]

21.5.label: Question 21.5
21.5.scenario:
A block of business has two years left until maturity. The cashflows (premiums plus investment
return less claims and expenses) expected at the end of the first and second years are 15 and –
100 respectively.

The insurer has assets of 120. The current reserve is 100. The insurer expects to hold a reserve of
110 at the end of the first year after allowing for the cashflow received at that time.
21.5.question: Calculate the embedded value of this block of business using a discount rate of 8% pa.

Recall that the embedded value has two p:          the net assets, which are the excess of the assets over the liabilities, ie 20 in this case          the present value of shareholder profits, and in each year, shareholder profits will be the cashflow less any increase in the reserves. At the end of the first year, the cashflow is 15, and the reserves increase by 10. Hence, the shareholder profit is 5. At the end of the second year, the cashflow is –100, but the reserves have decreased by 110 (the reserve is zero after the payment of the claims at the end of the policy). Hence, the shareholder profit is 10.
1.08 1.082: The embedded value is the sum of the net assets and the present value of the shareholder profit, and so in this case equals 33.2.

21.6.label: Question 21.6
21.6.scenario:
A proprietary health and care insurance company issues a wide range of conventional long‐term

without‐profits products.

Explain how the following will influence the company’s embedded value calculations:

(a).question: the general economic and commercial environment, and

The general economic and commercial envi: The investment return assumptions will be affected by current expectations about the future state of the economy (eg as reflected in the yields on long‐term bonds).                    [½]
The assumption about future inflation (of claims or expenses) will be affected by the expected long‐term rate of wage inflation.                                                              [½]
Any likely changes in tax rates or tax treatment of long‐term health and care insurance companies will affect the embedded value calculation.                                                   [½]
Changes in the regulatory environment, eg for calculating supervisory reserves, would need to be incorporated.                                                                                 [½]
Expected changes in economic prosperity will affect withdrawals and claims experience.              [½]
Persistency rates should also reflect media comment and the expected future levels of competition.                                                                                       [½]

Factors specific to the company’s business should be taken into account, eg the effect of possible poor selling practices in the past, and future changes in demand from one policy type to another. [½]
These can affect future persistency rates for existing business.                                   [½]
Current and expected future demand for, and supply of, capital will help determine the appropriate risk discount rate to use, …                                                           [½]
… along with the returns currently expected from risk‐free investments.                            [½]
Changes to the environment over time will cause the assumptions used in the calculations to change.                                                                                     [½]

(b).question:
the company’s ongoing experience                                                             [7]

Note: a definition of EV or a description of how it is calculated is not required, nor are details of
the individual investigations that may be carried out.

The company’s ongoing experience: The company’s experience is used to monitor the appropriateness of the assumptions used in the embedded value calculations.                                                                [½]
Experience includes:          rate of return earned on assets                                                           [¼]
Comparison of the actual experience against the assumptions used in the model enables the company to assess the continuing validity of those assumptions.                                    [½]
Any persistent discrepancy over time will lead to a revision of the embedded value basis.           [½]

The identification of trends over time, inconsistent with expectations, could also lead to revised assumptions.                                                                                      [½]
Monitoring can identify the sources of poor experience.                                                [½]
The remedial actions taken by the company can change the future experience in a significant way, which can again alter the embedded value assumptions.                                        [½]

21.7.label: Question 21.7
21.7.scenario: A health and care insurer is putting itself up for sale.
21.7.question: Suggest reasons why two different consultant actuaries may put different values on its reserves.

Solution: The actuaries may be working for different parties. For example, if hired by the seller, the actuary may have been asked to produce a low reserve value, giving a higher sale value.                  [½]
If hired by the purchaser, the actuary may have been asked to produce a more cautious value resulting in a high reserve value.                                                          [½]

However, it is more likely (and professional) that the consultant actuary should present the best estimate irrespective of who he or she works for, and that the company asking for the work builds in their own adjustments.                                                                       [½]
The actuaries may have different access to data.                                                       [½]
For example, less data would probably be available for the actuary working for a purchaser.            [½]
Where less data are available, prudence may have been built in to the calculations.                    [½]
The actuaries may use different reserving methods, eg different types of run‐off triangles.            [½]
The actuaries may have used different methods to value the assets, and so valued the reserves on a consistent (and therefore different) basis.                                                 [½]
One (or both) actuaries may have made a mistake.                                                       [½]
The purchaser will value the reserves in the context of what it intends to do with the purchased business, which will affect the reserve value.                                                  [½]
They may have different views on expenses, eg claims handling expenses …                               [½]
… or they may have treated expenses differently in the calculation of the reserves.                    [½]
The actuaries may have taken different views on the assumptions used to calculate the technical reserves.                                                                                    [½]
 trends in morbidity:          trends in mortality.                            [¼ for each valid parameter, maximum ½] For long‐term business the resulting reserves may be very sensitive to small changes in these assumptions.                                                                                  [½]
For short‐term business, many methods of reserving (eg triangulation methods) build subjectivity into the calculation, so slightly different assumptions are to be expected.                   [½]
For example, they may have assumed different economic assumptions, eg medical inflation – both past and future, …                                                                   [½]
… or they may have different views on the future of medical costs and fees.                       [½]
Reserves for short‐term business may have been discounted, although this may not make much difference, as health insurance is generally short‐tailed.                                [½]
The figures may have been calculated for other reasons, eg supervisory or internal purposes.      [½]
The actuaries may have used different assumptions about closure to new business, ...              [½]
... which would affect per‐policy expenses, selective lapsing etc.                           [½]
